description:
You are working as a performance-engineer and have just collected a small sample of latency measurements emitted by a micro-services application.

Directory layout that already exists when the session starts  
/home/user/perfdata/performance.json          – raw measurements (see structure below)  
/home/user/perfdata/schema.json               – JSON Schema describing the shape of performance.json  
/home/user/reports/                           – empty directory; you have write permission here  

Your assignment (5-10 shell commands should be enough):

1. Validate /home/user/perfdata/performance.json against /home/user/perfdata/schema.json.  
   • Use any CLI tool that can run in a standard Ubuntu container (examples: jq, python3 with jsonschema, ajv, etc.).  
   • If the JSON does **not** pass the schema, abort the task immediately and write the single line  
     VALIDATION_STATUS: FAILED  
     to the summary log (see step 4).  
   • If it **does** pass, continue with the remaining steps.

2. With jq (or any pure-shell alternative that does not require root privileges) extract only those “runs” whose response_time_ms value is **≥ 500**.  
   • Save the extracted array in /home/user/perfdata/slow_requests.json.  
   • The file must be valid JSON and contain only the list (array) of objects, *no* wrapper object.

3. Calculate the following five statistics using jq, awk, or pure bash arithmetic:  
   a. TOTAL_REQUESTS          – total number of elements in .runs  
   b. SLOW_REQUESTS           – total number of objects written to slow_requests.json  
   c. AVERAGE_RESPONSE_TIME_MS (rounded to two decimals) – across **all** runs  
   d. MAX_RESPONSE_TIME_MS    – highest response_time_ms value found  
   e. MAX_RESPONSE_SERVICE    – the service name that owns that max latency

4. Write exactly six lines to /home/user/reports/summary.log in the **order shown below** (no extra spaces, tabs, or blank lines). Each key, a colon + single space, then the value:

TOTAL_REQUESTS: <integer>
SLOW_REQUESTS: <integer>
AVERAGE_RESPONSE_TIME_MS: <decimal with two digits>
MAX_RESPONSE_TIME_MS: <integer>
MAX_RESPONSE_SERVICE: <string>
SLOW_SERVICES_LIST (>=500ms): <comma-separated unique service names in alphabetical order>

Example layout (values will differ):

TOTAL_REQUESTS: 42  
SLOW_REQUESTS: 10  
AVERAGE_RESPONSE_TIME_MS: 511.27  
MAX_RESPONSE_TIME_MS: 914  
MAX_RESPONSE_SERVICE: inventory  
SLOW_SERVICES_LIST (>=500ms): auth, payments

5. Display the contents of both /home/user/perfdata/slow_requests.json and /home/user/reports/summary.log to the console so the reviewer can quickly see your results.

Notes & constraints
• Do not modify performance.json or schema.json.  
• summary.log must be created even when validation fails (see step 1).  
• Only /home/user/reports/summary.log and /home/user/perfdata/slow_requests.json will be graded; ensure their exact path, spelling, line order, punctuation and numeric precision match the specification so automated tests can parse them.  
• Stay within normal user privileges; no sudo.  
• The whole task should be doable with roughly 5-10 commands (directory change not counted).
